segments.text: Welcome everybody.
Wonderful to have you all here. Thank you for joining us.
We've got you here for half an hour.
Sam Upend, our chair of the health committee,
having a number of you have already been in here
and spoken to us, so we appreciate you all coming
back, coming back together.
'cause coming together is what we need to do
to deliver for New Zealanders.
Um, I'll let you introduce yourself
and make a few, um, brief remarks.
And then I know members
around the table we'll have questions.
And I think the purpose of this is to, look,
we had a strategy back in 2017.
Um, we haven't seen it implemented.
We want to make sure that the reasons it wasn't implemented,
uh, sort of addressed so it doesn't happen again.
And then to move forward, right?
'cause we need to deliver better outcomes
for Kiwis in the space. Thank you very much.
Thank you. Thanks.
Um, Koto, I'm Alison Bennett, uh, G
of Health Systems Strategy at Man Water.

Don Helen Stokes Land from House New Zealand, and Dr.

We've got some other, um,
Rachel Haggerty here also from Health
New Zealand to help us today.
Um, I'd like to acknowledge that whilst we have a strong,
clear strategy, we haven't delivered as intended
and still have one
of the lowest donation rates internationally.
I think we have done a lot of work around a number
of the strategic priorities.
However, there are two remaining priorities
where we could do a lot more.
Um, we're really heartened around the work
that NZ Boss has done
and the work that HZ has been doing in terms
of improving capacity in the system, uh,
and also the work that's going on around the development
of the plan to deliver the strategic content.
So
Thank you. Um,
so, um, first of all, I'd like to thank Michael Papes
for raising this issue is a critical one for us,
and I think it's becoming more critical.
So I'll just capture that for the committee by saying that
both referral rates from hospital ICUs
for potential organ donors are declining quite
rapidly in the last two years.
Um, we sit at around 30%
or 30 to 40% here as compared to, um,
90% in Australia and the uk.
So in terms of donors that we are, potential donors
that we are notified about
and can have conversations with health about.
A similar issue is happening
with consent rates in New Zealand.
So when whanau are
formally approached about organ donation within our system,
between 30 and 40% say yes,
and that has decreased from 40 to 50% in more recent in,
in the relatively recent past.
Um, if you look at somewhere like Australia or the UK
or we're talking usually about 50
to 60% consent in that setting.
And that reflects really our ability to deliver.
Um, and it is based on really specific funding.
Our ability to deliver at the patient bedside
daily support from someone who understands

to help them make those numbers better.
And also can support clinicians who are currently
really having, uh, challenges delivering
what are really difficult processes moving
through the organ donation, uh, identification
and consent process.
It's not an easy process.
Both myself
and my colleague Joe, who's here with me, our intensivist
of 20 years standing each,
we know the processes, we know the issues.
So we know that that is what needs to be provided
and the funding has not been there to enable us to do that.
Um, and that is the piece that we're going to work on
and work forward, um,
and do some formal costing, some formal collaboration
with the Ministry and Health New Zealand to try
and make sure we understand exactly
what is required to deliver that.
Thank you. And very briefly, KO Helen sts.

for Health New Zealand, and my colleague branch of here

it's a large organization.

but I think this, uh, raising this issue in this way,

a strong spotlight on something.

it's nobody disagrees.
We're all absolutely united in that it's good for patients

It is sustainable. It is, uh, very cost effective.

Thank you. Thank you very much

source_metadata.description:
17 December 2025 | Health Committee

Briefing from Michael Papesch
Health New Zealand
	Dame Helen Stokes-Lampard, National Chief Medical Officer
	Rachel Haggerty, Director, Funding – Hospitals
Ministry of Health
	Allison Bennett, Group Manager, Health System Settings
	Peter Jane, Principal Advisor, Crown Entity Monitoring
	Saerom Shin, Senior Policy Analyst
New Zealand Blood Service
	Sam Cliffe, Chief Executive [Zoom]
	Sarah Morley, CMO
	Joanne Ritchie, Clinical Director, Organ Donation NZ
